metadata.summary: GitHub Actions CD patterns: staged deploys with environment protection, reusable workflows, composite actions, canary and blue-green strategies, and release automation with changelog generation.

content.title: GitHub Actions Deployment Patterns
content.summary: Structure CD pipelines as staged deploy jobs (staging auto, production manual approval) backed by GitHub Environments. Extract shared deploy logic into reusable workflows called with workflow_call and shared steps into composite actions. For production, implement canary (10% traffic, metrics gate, full rollout) or blue-green (swap load balancer after validation). Release workflows trigger on version tags and publish packages plus Docker images.

why.p: Ad-hoc deploy steps inlined in a single monolithic workflow are the root cause of most CD failures: no environment protection means one commit pushes directly to production; no concurrency control means two deploys race; no reuse means the same deploy logic is copied across 10 repos with inconsistent security settings. GitHub Environments provide approval gates, environment-scoped secrets, and deployment timelines out of the box — they are free for all public repos and included in Teams/Enterprise.

Reusable workflows (workflow_call) let you define deploy logic once in a centralised repo and call it from every service repo. Composite actions share steps within a single repo. Together they enforce consistency: every service gets the same OIDC configuration, the same health check, the same Slack notification format.

list.item: Any push to main that should automatically deploy to staging and optionally to production after review.
Organisations with multiple service repos that share the same deploy target (K8s cluster, ECS, Vercel org).
Release pipelines that must publish npm/PyPI packages and Docker images together on a version tag.
Workflows that need to deploy to more than two environments (dev, staging, prod) with different approval requirements.
Long-running batch jobs exceeding GHA's 6-hour job limit — use Argo Workflows or Airflow instead.
Deployments requiring shared mutable state between jobs — GHA jobs run on isolated runners; passing large state via artifacts is slow and fragile.
Heavy parallel fanout (1000+ jobs at once) — GHA concurrency limits and queuing cause stalls; use BuildKit cluster or Buildbarn.
Repos on GitLab/Bitbucket — a GHA mirror creates a dual source of truth and fragments PR review.

rule.statement: Production deploy jobs MUST use a GitHub Environment with required reviewers. Set cancel-in-progress: false on deploy concurrency groups so in-flight production deploys are never cancelled by a new commit.
rule.rationale: cancel-in-progress: true on a production deploy group kills an active rollout when a new commit is pushed — a partial deploy is worse than a delayed one.

rule.statement: Reusable workflows MUST pass only the secrets they need, not secrets: inherit. Each secret must be listed explicitly in the secrets: block with a description.
rule.rationale: secrets: inherit passes every secret in the calling repo's scope. Over-exposure of prod secrets to staging workflows violates least privilege and creates blast radius if the workflow is compromised.

rule.statement: Every job in a CD workflow MUST declare timeout-minutes. Use 15-30 for tests, 60 for builds, 120 for deploys. The default 360-minute timeout is too long and leaves runners occupied during an outage.
rule.rationale: A hung Kubernetes rollout will silently occupy a runner for 6 hours without a timeout. Explicit timeouts surface stuck deployments early and free runners for other work.

rule.statement: Reusable workflows MUST define outputs for any value downstream workflows need (e.g., deployment-url). Producing steps MUST have id: and the job MUST have an outputs: mapping.
rule.rationale: Agents commonly miss one half of the output chain: a step with id: but no job-level outputs: produces empty string for consumers, causing silent failures.

section.name: staged-cd-pipeline
section.p: A canonical CD pipeline: build Docker image → deploy to staging (auto) → deploy to production (manual approval). All environment secrets are scoped to their environment in GitHub Settings.

example.title: CD with staging auto-deploy and production approval

section.name: reusable-workflows
section.p: Extract the deploy logic into a reusable workflow (workflow_call) under .github/workflows/_reusable-deploy.yml. Callers pass environment, image-tag, cluster, and namespace as inputs.

example.title: Reusable deploy workflow with typed inputs
example.code:
# .github/workflows/_reusable-deploy.yml name: Reusable Deploy on: workflow_call: inputs: environment: required: true type: string image-tag: required: true type: string cluster-name: required: true type: string namespace: required: true type: string timeout: required: false type: string default: '10m' secrets: AWS_ROLE_ARN: required: true SLACK_WEBHOOK: required: false outputs: deployment-url: description: "URL of deployed environment" value: ${{ jobs.deploy.outputs.url }}
jobs: deploy: name: Deploy to ${{ inputs.environment }} runs-on: ubuntu-latest timeout-minutes: 120 outputs: url: ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.url }} environment: name: ${{ inputs.environment }} steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 with: role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }} aws-region: eu-central-1 - uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v4 - run: aws eks update-kubeconfig --name ${{ inputs.cluster-name }} --region eu-central-1 - id: deploy run: | kubectl set image deployment/app app=${{ inputs.image-tag }} -n ${{ inputs.namespace }} kubectl rollout status deployment/app -n ${{ inputs.namespace }} --timeout=${{ inputs.timeout }} echo "url=https://${{ inputs.environment }}.example.com" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT - if: always() uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v1.26.0 with: payload: '{"text": "Deploy to ${{ inputs.environment }}: ${{ job.status }}"}' env: SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }}

example.title: Caller using the reusable workflow
example.code:
# .github/workflows/deploy-all.yml jobs: staging: uses: ./.github/workflows/_reusable-deploy.yml with: environment: staging image-tag: ghcr.io/org/app:${{ github.sha }} cluster-name: staging-cluster namespace: staging secrets: AWS_ROLE_ARN: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }} SLACK_WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }}
production: needs: staging uses: ./.github/workflows/_reusable-deploy.yml with: environment: production image-tag: ghcr.io/org/app:${{ github.sha }} cluster-name: production-cluster namespace: production secrets: AWS_ROLE_ARN: ${{ secrets.AWS_PROD_ROLE_ARN }} SLACK_WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }}
example.note: Secrets passed explicitly, not via secrets: inherit. Prod uses AWS_PROD_ROLE_ARN, staging uses AWS_ROLE_ARN.

section.name: release-workflow
section.p: Release workflows trigger on version tags (v*) and publish packages, Docker images, and GitHub Releases with generated changelogs.

example.title: Release workflow: changelog + npm + Docker
example.code:
# .github/workflows/release.yml name: Release on: push: tags: ['v*'] permissions: contents: write packages: write
jobs: release: runs-on: ubuntu-latest timeout-minutes: 60 steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 with: { fetch-depth: 0 } - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: node-version: '20' cache: 'npm' registry-url: 'https://npm.pkg.github.com' - run: npm ci && npm run build - id: changelog uses: orhun/git-cliff-action@v3 with: config: cliff.toml args: --latest --strip header - uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2 with: body: ${{ steps.changelog.outputs.content }} draft: false prerelease: ${{ contains(github.ref, 'alpha') || contains(github.ref, 'beta') || contains(github.ref, 'rc') }} files: | dist/*.tar.gz dist/*.zip - run: npm publish env: NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }} - id: version run: echo "version=${GITHUB_REF#refs/tags/v}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3 - uses: docker/login-action@v3 with: registry: ghcr.io username: ${{ github.actor }} password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} - uses: docker/build-push-action@v6 with: context: . platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64 push: true tags: | ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ steps.version.outputs.version }} ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:latest cache-from: type=gha cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

section.name: composite-action
section.p: Composite actions share steps within a repo without the overhead of a full workflow. They live under .github/actions/{name}/action.yml.

example.title: Composite action template
example.code: # .github/actions/setup-node-app/action.yml name: 'Setup Node App' description: 'Checkout, setup Node, install deps with cache' inputs: node-version: description: 'Node.js version' required: false default: '20' outputs: cache-hit: description: 'Whether the cache was hit' value: ${{ steps.cache.outputs.cache-hit }} runs: using: 'composite' steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - id: cache uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }} cache: 'npm' - shell: bash run: npm ci

section.name: deployment-targets
section.p: Common deployment target patterns for SSH, Vercel, and Netlify.

example.title: SSH deploy
Vercel deploy

checklist.check: Production environment has required reviewers configured in GitHub Settings.
Deploy concurrency group uses cancel-in-progress: false.
timeout-minutes set on every deploy job (not default 360).
Reusable workflows pass secrets explicitly, not via secrets: inherit.
Reusable workflow outputs have both a step id: and a job-level outputs: mapping.
Release workflow triggered on tag push (v*), not on branch push.
Docker images tagged with both version and latest in release workflow.
